anonymized_passage: before uncertain demand is realized, before future demand by market is known, a capacity planner chooses first-stage capacity and a recourse policy using capacity costs and the demand distribution. The stated objective is to minimize capacity, recourse, and unmet-demand cost, subject to the condition that recourse actions occur only after demand is observed and must respect installed capacity. First-stage capacity changes the feasible and costly adjustments available after demand realization. More advance capacity is expensive but reduces shortage and emergency recourse exposure. The universally superior policy will transform the market and guarantee better performance. The analysis isolates when flexible recourse substitutes for advance capacity.

model_or_study_facts: recourse actions occur only after demand is observed and must respect installed capacity
decisions: chooses first-stage capacity and a recourse policy
context.objective: minimize capacity, recourse, and unmet-demand cost
decision_time_information: capacity costs and the demand distribution
constraints: recourse actions occur only after demand is observed and must respect installed capacity
context.mechanism: First-stage capacity changes the feasible and costly adjustments available after demand realization
supplied_evidence: Synthetic illustration; no external empirical evidence supplied.
